Authors: Zi-Qiang Zhong; Ding-Sheng Jiang; Saskia Erdmann; Yi-Xiang Chen; Xia Hu; Zheng Xu; Wei Zhang; Jia-Ru Sheng; Wang-Ye Li; Fang Huang

Journal: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta

DOI: 10.1016/j.gca.2026.07.002

Relevance: 0.645 (must_read)

The extent to which partial melting of subducting slabs contributes to arc magmatism beneath sub-arc regions in cold subduction zones remains highly debated. Traditional models emphasize that altered oceanic crust (AOC) undergoes dehydration, while subducted sediments undergo partial melting. However, an increasing number of studies have argued that partial melting of AOC may also be required to account for the trace-element budgets of arc lavas, particularly their elevated Sr concentrations. To investigate whether AOC undergoes partial melting beneath sub-arc regions, we report Rb isotope (δ87Rb) data for the Cretaceous arc basalts and back-arc basalts associated with the cold subduction zone of the Izanagi plate in Eastern China. The δ87Rb values of the basalts show clear variation across the subduction zone, increasing from −0.31 ± 0.45‰ (2SD, N = 9) for the arc basalts to −0.19 ± 0.07‰ (2SD, N = 22) for the back-arc basalts. This across-arc variation in δ87Rb indicates that while partial melting of subducted sediments occurs across the sub-arc and back-arc regions, partial melting of AOC is only evident in the back-arc region, not beneath the sub-arc region. Moreover, negative correlations between δ87Rb and Sr concentrations strongly suggest that the elevated Sr concentrations (385–913 μg/g) of the arc basalts resulted from the subduction input from sediments, rather than AOC melts as has recently been proposed for global data. Similar compositional relations are observed in arc lavas from other cold subduction zones (Izu-Bonin-Mariana and Tonga-Kermadec), which typically exhibit a negative correlation between Sr/Y and 143Nd/144Nd or Nb/U ratios. Together with recent δ87Rb data from the Mariana arc, our results indicate that in cold subduction zones, AOC may not necessarily undergo partial melting beneath the sub-arc region—even though subducted sediments do. Partial melts of metasomatic pyroxenites formed by interaction between sediment melts and the sub-arc mantle peridotites can carry a significant budget of trace elements, including Sr, contributing to arc magmas. The lack of partial melting in AOC beneath sub-arc regions permits the deep transport of water, carbon, and trace elements, with significant implications for mantle heterogeneity, carbon flux imbalance, and global geochemical cycling.

item.title: Paleoproterozoic Dongyaozhuang orogenic gold deposit in the Trans-North China Orogen: Constraints from in-situ U-Th-Pb dating of hydrothermal monazite and trace elements and sulfur isotopes of sulfides
item.dc:creator: Ying Ma
Shao-Yong Jiang
Ning Jin
Dao-Hui Pi
Yu Jiang
Guo-Hai Guo
item.description: The Wutai Complex in the Trans-North China Orogen hosts several significant Precambrian mesothermal, greenstone-hosted gold deposits. However, as in many Precambrian orogenic belts worldwide, these deposits are associated with complex hydrothermal systems involving multistage fluid activity during successive mineralization stages. As a result, their age, origin, evolutionary history, and genetic mechanisms remain poorly constrained. Here, we present in-situ hydrothermal monazite U-Th-Pb geochronological data, together with trace-element and S isotope analyses of multiple generations of pyrite and arsenopyrite, from the representative greenstone-hosted Dongyaozhuang gold deposit in the Wutai Complex. These integrated datasets provide new constraints on the hydrothermal evolution and genesis of the deposit. Gold mineralization occurred in three distinct stages and is represented by veinlet-hosted and disseminated ores: Stage I disseminated pyrite-arsenopyrite assemblages, Stage II quartz-carbonate-pyrite veinlets, and Stage III quartz-chalcopyrite-pyrite veinlets. Six generations of pyrite were identified, including Py1 from Stage I, Py2a, Py2b, and Py2c from Stage II, and Py3a and Py3b from Stage III. Hydrothermal monazite from Stage II gold-mineralized veinlets yielded a mean 232Th/208Pb age of 1846.7 ± 4.7 Ma, which is interpreted as the timing of gold mineralization. This age is inconsistent with regional granitic magmatism at 2560–2515 Ma, 2180–2120 Ma, and ∼1800 Ma, but overlaps with metamorphic and deformational processes related to the Paleoproterozoic orogeny. Most of the gold was precipitated during Stage II, when pyrite commonly developed a complex core-mantle-rim texture. Most sulfide generations show a narrow range of δ34SV-CDT values from −3.7 ‰ to 2.6 ‰, consistent with sulfur mobilization from greenstone wall rocks and its subsequent reprecipitation at the site of ore formation. In contrast, the negative δ34SV-CDT values of Py2a (−6.7 to −2.3 ‰) suggest isotopic fractionation induced by an increase in the pH of the metamorphic ore-forming fluid. These results, together with the geological evidence, indicate that the Dongyaozhuang deposit is an orogenic gold deposit genetically related to regional metamorphism and orogenic deformation associated with the Paleoproterozoic continent-arc-continent collision between the Western and Eastern Blocks. Published: 2026-07-04T01:02:54+00:00 Venue: Precambrian Research Score: 0.630 (must_read)

Authors: Ying Ma; Shao-Yong Jiang; Ning Jin; Dao-Hui Pi; Yu Jiang; Guo-Hai Guo

Journal: Precambrian Research

DOI: 10.1016/j.precamres.2026.108199

Relevance: 0.630 (must_read)

The Wutai Complex in the Trans-North China Orogen hosts several significant Precambrian mesothermal, greenstone-hosted gold deposits. However, as in many Precambrian orogenic belts worldwide, these deposits are associated with complex hydrothermal systems involving multistage fluid activity during successive mineralization stages. As a result, their age, origin, evolutionary history, and genetic mechanisms remain poorly constrained. Here, we present in-situ hydrothermal monazite U-Th-Pb geochronological data, together with trace-element and S isotope analyses of multiple generations of pyrite and arsenopyrite, from the representative greenstone-hosted Dongyaozhuang gold deposit in the Wutai Complex. These integrated datasets provide new constraints on the hydrothermal evolution and genesis of the deposit. Gold mineralization occurred in three distinct stages and is represented by veinlet-hosted and disseminated ores: Stage I disseminated pyrite-arsenopyrite assemblages, Stage II quartz-carbonate-pyrite veinlets, and Stage III quartz-chalcopyrite-pyrite veinlets. Six generations of pyrite were identified, including Py1 from Stage I, Py2a, Py2b, and Py2c from Stage II, and Py3a and Py3b from Stage III. Hydrothermal monazite from Stage II gold-mineralized veinlets yielded a mean 232Th/208Pb age of 1846.7 ± 4.7 Ma, which is interpreted as the timing of gold mineralization. This age is inconsistent with regional granitic magmatism at 2560–2515 Ma, 2180–2120 Ma, and ∼1800 Ma, but overlaps with metamorphic and deformational processes related to the Paleoproterozoic orogeny. Most of the gold was precipitated during Stage II, when pyrite commonly developed a complex core-mantle-rim texture. Most sulfide generations show a narrow range of δ34SV-CDT values from −3.7 ‰ to 2.6 ‰, consistent with sulfur mobilization from greenstone wall rocks and its subsequent reprecipitation at the site of ore formation. In contrast, the negative δ34SV-CDT values of Py2a (−6.7 to −2.3 ‰) suggest isotopic fractionation induced by an increase in the pH of the metamorphic ore-forming fluid. These results, together with the geological evidence, indicate that the Dongyaozhuang deposit is an orogenic gold deposit genetically related to regional metamorphism and orogenic deformation associated with the Paleoproterozoic continent-arc-continent collision between the Western and Eastern Blocks.

item.title: Multiple Au enrichment processes in the Early Cretaceous Heilongtan–Xiejiagou deposit, Tongbai Orogen, Central China
item.dc:creator: Jun-Jie Zhou
Zhan-Ke Li
Mei-Zhen Yang
Da Ke
Shao-Rui Zhao
Ke-Qing Zong
Zaicong Wang
Bo Zu
Le-Le Ding
item.description: The Tongbai Orogen is an important Au province in central China, but the processes responsible for localized high-grade Au enrichment in its southern segment remain poorly constrained. Here, we investigate the Heilongtan–Xiejiagou Au deposit using ore geology, hand-specimen-scale element mapping, ore-stage geochronology, and in-situ trace-element and S–Pb isotope analyses of sulfides. The deposit records three stages of mineralization: Stage I quartz–sericite–pyrite alteration, Stage II quartz–pyrite vein, and Stage III quartz–polymetallic sulfide vein. Ore-stage geochronology based on Rb-Sr and U-Pb isotopic systems of Stage I sericite and monazite, and Stage III muscovite constrains the main Au–Ag mineralization to ca. 140–134 Ma. The S–Pb isotope data, together with the Early Cretaceous ages and spatial association with granitoid magmatism, indicate a major magmatic–hydrothermal contribution, with variable input from Neoproterozoic host rocks. Pyrite records a systematic evolution from As-rich invisible-Au-bearing Py-I, through porous and inclusion-rich Py-II associated with Au redistribution and polymetallic enrichment, to trace-element-poor Py-III associated with late polymetallic sulfides. These features indicate that Stage II was the main stage of Au remobilization and local reconcentration. During this stage, coupled dissolution–reprecipitation of earlier auriferous pyrite, together with additional Au input from the infiltrating fluid, generated porous reaction domains that promoted Au release, redistribution, and electrum precipitation. These results link the deposit to Early Cretaceous magmatic–hydrothermal activity and suggest that exploration in the southern Tongbai Orogen should focus on reactivated vein systems capable of localized high-grade upgrading. Overall, this study provides new insight into fluid-mediated remobilization and local reconcentration of pyrite-hosted Au in high-grade lode gold systems. Published: 2026-07-03T23:08:06+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.620 (consider)

The Tongbai Orogen is an important Au province in central China, but the processes responsible for localized high-grade Au enrichment in its southern segment remain poorly constrained. Here, we investigate the Heilongtan–Xiejiagou Au deposit using ore geology, hand-specimen-scale element mapping, ore-stage geochronology, and in-situ trace-element and S–Pb isotope analyses of sulfides. Ore-stage geochronology based on Rb-Sr and U-Pb isotopic systems of Stage I sericite and monazite, and Stage III muscovite constrains the main Au–Ag mineralization to ca. 140–134 Ma. During this stage, coupled dissolution–reprecipitation of earlier auriferous pyrite, together with additional Au input from the infiltrating fluid, generated porous reaction domains that promoted Au release, redistribution, and electrum precipitation. These results link the deposit to Early Cretaceous magmatic–hydrothermal activity and suggest that exploration in the southern Tongbai Orogen should focus on reactivated vein systems capable of localized high-grade upgrading. Overall, this study provides new insight into fluid-mediated remobilization and local reconcentration of pyrite-hosted Au in high-grade lode gold systems.

item.title: Gold mineralisation characteristics of the typical ore body and the potential for exploration in the Qiucun Au deposit, Dehua county, Fujian province, China
item.dc:creator: Fei-Peng Fan
Pei Ni
Fan Xiao
Yan Zhou
Guang-Fu Xing
Zi-Hao Zhao
Zhi-Qiang Yu
Ming-Seng Fan
Nan Wang
item.description: The Qiucun Au deposit is located in Dehua County, Fujian Province, within the Wuyi Mountain metallogenic belt. Gold mineralisation of the typical ore body exhibits distinct zoning characteristics between 940 m and 647 m, is structurally controlled by a volcanic conduit and its associated fracture zones, and is closely associated with quartz (chalcedony)-sericite-pyrite-calcite assemblages. To decipher the ore-forming processes of the Qiucun deposit, detailed geological investigations were carried out, along with sulfur isotope analysis of syn-ore pyrite, microthermometric analysis (homogenization temperature and salinity) of fluid inclusions in syn-ore quartz, and U-Pb dating of syn-ore calcite. The results show that in typical orebodies, gold grade increases and then decreases from shallow to deeper sections. Gold occurs mainly as fracture-fillings within pyrite, displaying diverse morphologies and colours that correlate with grade. Significant gold mineralisation is hosted within sericite alteration zones, whereas the presence of calcite is generally associated with lower gold grades. The δ 34 S values of gold-related pyrite range from –6.8‰ to + 1.6‰, suggesting a deep magmatic source for the ore-forming materials. Fluid inclusions in quartz are dominated by two-phase (liquid-rich) types with minor evidence of boiling assemblages, obtaining low-temperature (180–260 °C) and low-salinity (0.35–5.56 wt% NaCl equiv.). U-Pb dating of calcite yields an age of 152.0 ± 17 Ma. Integrated evidence indicates that the Qiucun low-sulfidation epithermal Au deposit is related to the subduction of the paleo-Pacific Plate. Fractured volcanic breccia zones served as the main pathways for hydrothermal fluid migration and ore precipitation. Pyrite, sericite, calcite, and other minerals at different elevations are the main indicators for further gold exploration in the Qiucun gold deposit and its adjacent areas. Published: 2026-07-06T23:29:31+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.620 (consider)

Fractured volcanic breccia zones served as the main pathways for hydrothermal fluid migration and ore precipitation. item.title: The Preservation of Intragranular and Intergranular Coesite in Metasedimentary Rocks: Insights From a TEM‐Based Structure Analysis
item.dc:creator: Jinyu Zheng
Tao Chen
Junfeng Zhang
Penglei Liu
Wenlong Liu
Feng Shi
Mingxing Gong
Zhe Gong
item.description: ABSTRACT Coesite is a key indicator mineral of ultrahigh‐pressure (UHP) metamorphism. A “dry” environment is widely recognized as a prerequisite for coesite preservation during exhumation. However, the deformation behaviour of coesite, its retrograde replacement by quartz and their host and surrounding minerals remain poorly constrained at the micro‐ to nanoscale, especially for coesite included in or surrounded by nonrigid minerals. Here, we employed electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD), focused ion beam (FIB) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) to characterize the micro‐nanostructure of intragranular coesite (within dolomite) and intergranular coesite (surrounded by dolomite and apatite) in a UHP metasedimentary rock from the Ganjialing area, Dabie Shan region in east‐central China. Our observations reveal that both intragranular and intergranular coesite grains are essentially undeformed and dislocation‐free, whereas the palisade quartz rim enveloping coesite exhibits abundant dislocations and tangles. Based on the dislocation densities within these quartz rims, the differential stress is estimated at 347–663 MPa for intragranular coesite and 320–741 MPa for intergranular coesite grains, respectively. Combining this differential stress estimate with the regional P–T–t path constrains the corresponding metamorphic conditions to 715°C–720°C and 1.7–2.0 GPa. Furthermore, dolomite and apatite domains adjacent to coesite show higher dislocation densities than those distal to coesite. Collectively, the palisade quartz rim, dolomite and apatite form a composite buffer zone that not only maintains a closed, dry system but also accommodates the high differential stress generated by phase transformation. This buffer system effectively suppresses the replacement of coesite by quartz during decompression. Our findings demonstrate that transformation‐induced differential stress plays a pivotal role in the preservation of coesite within the mechanically weak, nonrigid matrix of UHP metasedimentary rocks during exhumation. item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jinyu Zheng; Tao Chen; Junfeng Zhang; Penglei Liu; Wenlong Liu; Feng Shi; Mingxing Gong; Zhe Gong

Journal: Journal of Metamorphic Geology

DOI: 10.1111/jmg.70046

Relevance: 0.617 (consider)

ABSTRACT Coesite is a key indicator mineral of ultrahigh‐pressure (UHP) metamorphism. A “dry” environment is widely recognized as a prerequisite for coesite preservation during exhumation. However, the deformation behaviour of coesite, its retrograde replacement by quartz and their host and surrounding minerals remain poorly constrained at the micro‐ to nanoscale, especially for coesite included in or surrounded by nonrigid minerals. Here, we employed electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD), focused ion beam (FIB) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) to characterize the micro‐nanostructure of intragranular coesite (within dolomite) and intergranular coesite (surrounded by dolomite and apatite) in a UHP metasedimentary rock from the Ganjialing area, Dabie Shan region in east‐central China. Our observations reveal that both intragranular and intergranular coesite grains are essentially undeformed and dislocation‐free, whereas the palisade quartz rim enveloping coesite exhibits abundant dislocations and tangles. Based on the dislocation densities within these quartz rims, the differential stress is estimated at 347–663 MPa for intragranular coesite and 320–741 MPa for intergranular coesite grains, respectively. Combining this differential stress estimate with the regional P–T–t path constrains the corresponding metamorphic conditions to 715°C–720°C and 1.7–2.0 GPa. Furthermore, dolomite and apatite domains adjacent to coesite show higher dislocation densities than those distal to coesite. Collectively, the palisade quartz rim, dolomite and apatite form a composite buffer zone that not only maintains a closed, dry system but also accommodates the high differential stress generated by phase transformation. This buffer system effectively suppresses the replacement of coesite by quartz during decompression. Our findings demonstrate that transformation‐induced differential stress plays a pivotal role in the preservation of coesite within the mechanically weak, nonrigid matrix of UHP metasedimentary rocks during exhumation.

item.title: Niobium and heavy rare earth element mineralization in the Bayan Obo deposit, China: insights from aeschynite mineralogy, geochemistry, and geochronology
item.dc:creator: Qing Sun
Xian-Hua Li
item.description: The Bayan Obo deposit in China is the largest light rare earth element (LREE) deposit worldwide, and also hosts substantial Nb and heavy REE (HREE) resources. However, the timing and enrichment mechanisms of the Nb and HREE mineralization are poorly constrained. Aeschynite is the most important Nb- and HREE-bearing mineral at Bayan Obo and was investigated in this study to reconstruct the history of Nb and HREE mineralization. In situ Lu–Hf isochron dating of two types of aeschynite yielded consistent ages of 435–420 Ma, which overlap with the Th–Pb isotopic ages of intergrown monazite (438–392 Ma). These results demonstrate that early Paleozoic is a significant episode for Nb and HREE mineralization. The aeschynite has a close spatiotemporal relationship with alkali silicates, suggesting that hydrothermal fluids were naturally rich in alkalis and dominantly exsolved from coeval carbonatitic magmas. Strontium isotopic compositions of aeschynite are relatively enriched and exhibit an increased trend from the type-1 (87Sr/86Sr = 0.7037–0.7064) to type-2 (87Sr/86Sr = 0.7072–0.7128), which was likely caused by heterogeneous crustal contamination during the evolution of hydrothermal fluids. Paragenetic aeschynite, calcite, fluorite and bastnäsite reflect that carbonate (CO3 2–) and fluoride (F-) anions played a key role for Nb mobilization. The unloading of Nb in aeschynite was usually related to the variation of Ti content in hydrothermal fluids. According to apparent correlation of REE, Th and Ca in aeschynite and developed ilmenite, Nb unloading was influenced by the processes of coupled substitution or ilmenite crystallization. The enrichment of HREE was ascribed to preferential LREE fractionation following gradual temperature decrease of CO3 2–-bearing alkaline hydrothermal fluids. Moreover, earlier crystallized LREE minerals is potential trigger for local elevation of HREE content. Published: 2026-07-04T22:23:19+00:00 Venue: Geoscience Frontiers Score: 0.615 (consider)

Authors: Qing Sun; Xian-Hua Li

Journal: Geoscience Frontiers

DOI: 10.1016/j.gsf.2026.102405

Relevance: 0.615 (consider)

The Bayan Obo deposit in China is the largest light rare earth element (LREE) deposit worldwide, and also hosts substantial Nb and heavy REE (HREE) resources. However, the timing and enrichment mechanisms of the Nb and HREE mineralization are poorly constrained. Aeschynite is the most important Nb- and HREE-bearing mineral at Bayan Obo and was investigated in this study to reconstruct the history of Nb and HREE mineralization. In situ Lu–Hf isochron dating of two types of aeschynite yielded consistent ages of 435–420 Ma, which overlap with the Th–Pb isotopic ages of intergrown monazite (438–392 Ma). These results demonstrate that early Paleozoic is a significant episode for Nb and HREE mineralization. The aeschynite has a close spatiotemporal relationship with alkali silicates, suggesting that hydrothermal fluids were naturally rich in alkalis and dominantly exsolved from coeval carbonatitic magmas. Strontium isotopic compositions of aeschynite are relatively enriched and exhibit an increased trend from the type-1 (87Sr/86Sr = 0.7037–0.7064) to type-2 (87Sr/86Sr = 0.7072–0.7128), which was likely caused by heterogeneous crustal contamination during the evolution of hydrothermal fluids. Paragenetic aeschynite, calcite, fluorite and bastnäsite reflect that carbonate (CO3 2–) and fluoride (F-) anions played a key role for Nb mobilization. The unloading of Nb in aeschynite was usually related to the variation of Ti content in hydrothermal fluids. According to apparent correlation of REE, Th and Ca in aeschynite and developed ilmenite, Nb unloading was influenced by the processes of coupled substitution or ilmenite crystallization. The enrichment of HREE was ascribed to preferential LREE fractionation following gradual temperature decrease of CO3 2–-bearing alkaline hydrothermal fluids. Moreover, earlier crystallized LREE minerals is potential trigger for local elevation of HREE content.

item.title: Mélange melting generates metasomatic precursors for post-collisional potassic magmas
item.dc:creator: Shidong Guan
Mingdi Gao
Yu Wang
item.description: The significant role of mélanges in the sources of various mantle-derived magmas, particularly those recording crust-mantle interactions, has been increasingly debated. However, previous experimental studies have predominantly focused on their contribution to arc magmatism, leaving the links between mélange melting and postcollisional orogenic potassic magmatism poorly constrained. Here, we conducted high-temperature and high-pressure experiments simulating the partial melting of mélange rocks—consisting of continental crustal sediment, terrigenous blueschist, and depleted peridotite—at relatively shallow mantle conditions (e.g., at 1.5–2.0 GPa and 750–1150 °C). Our results demonstrate that the mass ratios of different lithologies within the mélange critically govern the major element compositions of the resulting melts, effectively distinguishing the petrogenetic pathways of postcollisional K-rich magmas from typical primitive arc magmas. Furthermore, rather than directly representing the final erupted magmas, our experimental melts act as pristine metasomatic agents. They represent the primary stage of crust-mantle interaction during shallow imbrication, where they ascend to refertilize the overlying lithospheric mantle. The subsequent partial melting of this newly enriched mantle—driven by postcollisional lithospheric dynamics—ultimately generates the final erupted K-rich magmas with their distinct geochemical signatures. Published: 2026-07-01T23:30:45+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.612 (consider)

item.title: Devonian Jinshuikou pegmatites at the northern margin of the Tibetan Plateau: UPb and SmNd constraints on their genetic relationship with regional granitoids
item.dc:creator: Si-Qi Liu
Shao-Yong Jiang
Mostafa Fayek
Hui-Min Su
Yu-Ying Che
Wei Wang
Qinglin Xia
item.description: Granitic pegmatites represent critical repositories of strategic rare metals (e.g., Li, Be, Nb, Ta) and are conventionally interpreted as terminal products of highly evolved granitic magmas. However, this classic genetic model was frequently challenged where pronounced age gaps exist between pegmatites and their putative host granitoids. Along the northern margin of the Tibetan Plateau lies a world-class lithium metallogenic belt hosting approximately 14.8 million tons of Li2O resources. As a key segment of this belt, the East Kunlun Orogen contains widespread granitoids and newly recognized pegmatite dikes in the Jinshuikou area, yet their emplacement ages and genetic linkage between each other remain poorly constrained. Here we present integrated in situ UPb geochronology, mineral chemistry, and SmNd isotope data for monazite, garnet, and columbite group minerals (CGMs) from the pegmatites to resolve these issues. UPb dating of monazite and CGMs yields consistent ages of 372–375 Ma, firmly establishing a Devonian crystallization age for the pegmatites. Despite a ~ 15 Myr age difference relative to the youngest coeval Devonian granitoids (ca. 389 Ma), SmNd isotopic systematics strongly support a genetic connection between them. Monazite from pegmatites yields a SmNd isochron age of 390.2 ± 6.0 Ma and εNd(t) values (−6.01 to −5.39) identical to those of coeval I-type granitoids in the region. These data indicate that the pegmatites and associated granitoids originated from a common deep crustal magma reservoir generated by partial melting of ancient continental crust with limited input from juvenile mantle materials. The pegmatites represent the final products of protracted magmatic evolution within a long-lived crustal magma chamber. This study not only documents a previously overlooked Paleozoic rare-metal pegmatite system in the East Kunlun Orogen but also demonstrates that age gaps alone do not negate a cogenetic relationship between pegmatites and regional granitoids, with important implications for rare-metal exploration models in orogenic belts. Published: 2026-07-06T23:28:42+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.600 (consider)

Relevance: 0.600 (consider)

Granitic pegmatites represent critical repositories of strategic rare metals (e.g., Li, Be, Nb, Ta) and are conventionally interpreted as terminal products of highly evolved granitic magmas. However, this classic genetic model was frequently challenged where pronounced age gaps exist between pegmatites and their putative host granitoids. Along the northern margin of the Tibetan Plateau lies a world-class lithium metallogenic belt hosting approximately 14.8 million tons of Li2O resources. As a key segment of this belt, the East Kunlun Orogen contains widespread granitoids and newly recognized pegmatite dikes in the Jinshuikou area, yet their emplacement ages and genetic linkage between each other remain poorly constrained. Here we present integrated in situ UPb geochronology, mineral chemistry, and SmNd isotope data for monazite, garnet, and columbite group minerals (CGMs) from the pegmatites to resolve these issues. UPb dating of monazite and CGMs yields consistent ages of 372–375 Ma, firmly establishing a Devonian crystallization age for the pegmatites. Despite a ~ 15 Myr age difference relative to the youngest coeval Devonian granitoids (ca. 389 Ma), SmNd isotopic systematics strongly support a genetic connection between them. Monazite from pegmatites yields a SmNd isochron age of 390.2 ± 6.0 Ma and εNd(t) values (−6.01 to −5.39) identical to those of coeval I-type granitoids in the region. These data indicate that the pegmatites and associated granitoids originated from a common deep crustal magma reservoir generated by partial melting of ancient continental crust with limited input from juvenile mantle materials. The pegmatites represent the final products of protracted magmatic evolution within a long-lived crustal magma chamber. This study not only documents a previously overlooked Paleozoic rare-metal pegmatite system in the East Kunlun Orogen but also demonstrates that age gaps alone do not negate a cogenetic relationship between pegmatites and regional granitoids, with important implications for rare-metal exploration models in orogenic belts.

item.title: Linking Himalayan uplift to crustal deformation: Structural and multi-chronometer constraints from the Kangmar dome
item.dc:creator: Zhen Li
Pengfei Li
Dewen Zheng
Fred Jourdan
Gideon Rosenbaum
Qiang Wang
item.description: The highest topography on Earth occurs in the Himalayan orogen, which is the quintessential example of continental collision. To elucidate the crustal processes that accompanied the rise of the Himalayas, we investigated the deformational history of the Kangmar dome in the eastern Himalayas. By integrating field mapping with monazite U-Th-Pb, mica 40Ar/39Ar, and zircon and apatite (UTh)/He geochronology, we identified three distinct phases of deformation. The first phase involved north-south contractional deformation at ca. 51–44 Ma. This was followed by pervasive top-to-north ductile shearing at ca. 26–17 Ma, which exhumed deep-crustal rocks. North-south shortening was responsible for the final doming, at ca. 15–8 Ma, which involved two distinct cooling and exhumation stages: an earlier bottom-up cooling event that was driven by underthrusting of the Tethyan Himalayan Sequence below the Kangmar dome, and a later pop-up exhumation of the dome core. These deformation phases, which were driven by India-Asia convergence, are correlated with structural patterns across the Himalayan orogen. The deformation record is temporally linked to the episodic uplift history of the Himalayas, thus elucidating potential driving mechanisms. Early Eocene contraction was likely responsible for elevating the Himalayas above sea level. The main rise of the Himalayas, which occurred during the Early Miocene, was possibly triggered by the southward migration of deep-crustal rocks toward the southern Himalayan front. Collectively, the deformation history of the Kangmar dome provides critical insights into the tectonic evolution of this collisional orogen, including its uplift history. Published: 2026-07-05T21:54:24+00:00 Venue: Earth-Science Reviews Score: 0.596 (consider)

By integrating field mapping with monazite U-Th-Pb, mica 40Ar/39Ar, and zircon and apatite (UTh)/He geochronology, we identified three distinct phases of deformation. The first phase involved north-south contractional deformation at ca. 51–44 Ma. This was followed by pervasive top-to-north ductile shearing at ca. 26–17 Ma, which exhumed deep-crustal rocks. North-south shortening was responsible for the final doming, at ca. 15–8 Ma, which involved two distinct cooling and exhumation stages: an earlier bottom-up cooling event that was driven by underthrusting of the Tethyan Himalayan Sequence below the Kangmar dome, and a later pop-up exhumation of the dome core. These deformation phases, which were driven by India-Asia convergence, are correlated with structural patterns across the Himalayan orogen. The deformation record is temporally linked to the episodic uplift history of the Himalayas, thus elucidating potential driving mechanisms. Early Eocene contraction was likely responsible for elevating the Himalayas above sea level. The main rise of the Himalayas, which occurred during the Early Miocene, was possibly triggered by the southward migration of deep-crustal rocks toward the southern Himalayan front. Collectively, the deformation history of the Kangmar dome provides critical insights into the tectonic evolution of this collisional orogen, including its uplift history.

item.title: Comment on “Super-reduced and ultra-high pressure minerals in ophiolites: A critical review and the case for meteorite impact” by
item.dc:creator: Zhiyun Lu
Jingsui Yang
item.description: Su and Jing (2025) hypothesized that meteorite impacts could have formed ultra-reduced and ultra-high-pressure minerals (especially microdiamonds) within ophiolites. Although competing genetic models enrich academic debate, systematic comparison between impact diamonds (from Popigai, Kara, Ukrainian craters and ureilite meteorites) and ophiolite-hosted diamonds reveals fundamental discrepancies across five key indices: crystal morphology, internal microstructures, nitrogen impurity characteristics, carbon isotope signatures and tectonic settings. Comprehensive mineralogical, geochemical and geological observations collectively rule out an impact origin for ophiolite-hosted diamonds. Published: 2026-07-01T06:39:22+00:00 Venue: Earth-Science Reviews Score: 0.595 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zhiyun Lu; Jingsui Yang

Journal: Earth-Science Reviews

DOI: 10.1016/j.earscirev.2026.105606

Relevance: 0.595 (consider)

Su and Jing (2025) hypothesized that meteorite impacts could have formed ultra-reduced and ultra-high-pressure minerals (especially microdiamonds) within ophiolites. Although competing genetic models enrich academic debate, systematic comparison between impact diamonds (from Popigai, Kara, Ukrainian craters and ureilite meteorites) and ophiolite-hosted diamonds reveals fundamental discrepancies across five key indices: crystal morphology, internal microstructures, nitrogen impurity characteristics, carbon isotope signatures and tectonic settings. Comprehensive mineralogical, geochemical and geological observations collectively rule out an impact origin for ophiolite-hosted diamonds.

item.title: Interface Deformation, Exhumation, and Underplating: The Role of Plate Velocity Changes Throughout the Evolution of a Subduction Zone
item.dc:creator: Valeria Turino
Adam F. Holt
Derek J. Neuharth
Ryan K. Stoner
Whitney M. Behr
item.description: Abstract Exhumed metamorphic rocks yield critical insights into the mechanical, chemical, and thermal structure of the plate interface that can be used to infer exhumation pathways and their evolution throughout a subduction zone's lifetime. Metamorphosed oceanic rocks often record exhumation and underplating over relatively punctuated time periods, which appear to be linked to specific stages of subduction. Although plate velocities also vary from one stage of subduction to the next, the influence of this time dependence on exhumation and underplating remains unclear. We therefore use 2‐D time‐dependent subduction models to investigate how evolving plate velocities affect the timing and distribution of exhumation and underplating. By extracting plate velocities from a freely evolving subduction model and imposing them on kinematically driven models, we isolate the effects of realistic velocity variations on interface processes without the complexity of a dynamically evolving slab. We also explore the role of interface strength by varying its rheology and composition. In our models, exhumation consistently initiates during a punctuated detachment event following decreases in subduction velocity. Shallow exhumation ( Published: 2026-07-03T18:01:01+00:00 Venue: Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems Score: 0.592 (consider)

item.title: Tracing hydrothermal fluid evolution of the Tongshankou skarn–porphyry Cu–Mo–W deposit (Eastern China) using integrated infrared spectroscopic characteristics and magnetic susceptibility
item.dc:creator: Shili Peng
Shitao Zhang
Hao Xu
Gaobin Chu
Longfei He
Yu Zhang
Hua Zhang
Rucao Li
Yihui Lyu
Di Ding
Siyuan Zhang
Haisen Huang
item.description: The Middle–Lower Yangtze River Metallogenic Belt (MLYRB) is one of the dominant skarn–porphyry Cu–Au metallogenic belts in Eastern China. Within this belt, skarn–porphyry Cu–Au deposits show a close genetic linkage to Late Mesozoic high‑K calc‑alkaline granitoids, and are associated with variable degrees of critical metals mineralization (i.e., Co, Te, Se and Re). Previous studies have generally suggested that these granitoids possess limited W metallogenic potential; nevertheless, several concealed medium‑to‑large W–Cu–Mo deposits have been discovered in their peripheral areas in recent years. At present, the hydrothermal fluid evolution of these W‑rich skarn–porphyry Cu polymetallic deposits remains poorly constrained, and their deep exploration indicators still need further optimization. This study focuses on the Tongshankou skarn–porphyry Cu–Mo–W deposit in the westernmost segment of the MLYRB. The deposit hosts contained metal resources of 0.55 Mt Cu, 0.01 Mt Mo, and 0.012 Mt WO3, with ore grades of 0.86 wt%, 0.10 wt%, and 0.19 wt%, respectively. By combining detailed petrographic observations with short-wave infrared (SWIR) and thermal infrared (TIR) spectral alteration mapping, this study pinpoints the precise ore-forming stages and delineates detailed alteration zoning features in the Tongshankou Cu–Mo–W deposit. Building on SWIR and TIR spectral parameters, alongside volumetric bulk magnetic susceptibility (Kbulk ) measurements, we propose four mineral exploration indicators for this deposit: (i) the widespread development of kaolinite, dickite, skarn, and retrograde alteration mineral assemblages; (ii) Fe-rich chlorite, identifiable by its characteristic absorption wavelength with Pos2250 > 2250 nm; (iii) white mica–montmorillonite, distinguished by a diagnostic absorption wavelength with Pos2200 1.0 × 10-3SI). Notably, the presence of kaolinite and dickite along the intrusive contact zones between ore-forming porphyries and carbonate wall rocks provides direct evidence for acidic fluid activity during the Cu–Mo–W mineralization process. The development of acid fluid conditions in the system is tightly linked to hydrogen ion release during the precipitation of iron oxides and sulfide minerals. Collectively, these results reveal that hydrothermal fluids associated with Cu–Mo–W mineralization in the Tongshankou deposit evolved from alkaline to acidic throughout the mineralization process. Combined with previous research advances, the present findings further suggest that acidic hydrothermal fluids likely represent a ubiquitous component of skarn-hosted Cu–Mo–W mineralization systems. Clay indicator minerals, including kaolinite and dickite, provide robust constraints on the hydrothermal fluid evolution of Tongshankou deposit and serve as newly recognized, effective exploration proxies for analogous skarn–porphyry Cu–Mo–W deposits worldwide. Significantly, this study demonstrates that the integrated application of SWIR and TIR spectroscopic analyses combined with magnetic susceptibility measurements provides a rigorous and reliable approach for identifying concealed skarn–porphyry ore bodies across the MLYRB, Eastern China. Published: 2026-07-05T22:00:24+00:00 Venue: Journal of Asian Earth Sciences Score: 0.591 (consider)

Previous studies have generally suggested that these granitoids possess limited W metallogenic potential; nevertheless, several concealed medium‑to‑large W–Cu–Mo deposits have been discovered in their peripheral areas in recent years. At present, the hydrothermal fluid evolution of these W‑rich skarn–porphyry Cu polymetallic deposits remains poorly constrained, and their deep exploration indicators still need further optimization. This study focuses on the Tongshankou skarn–porphyry Cu–Mo–W deposit in the westernmost segment of the MLYRB. The deposit hosts contained metal resources of 0.55 Mt Cu, 0.01 Mt Mo, and 0.012 Mt WO3, with ore grades of 0.86 wt%, 0.10 wt%, and 0.19 wt%, respectively. By combining detailed petrographic observations with short-wave infrared (SWIR) and thermal infrared (TIR) spectral alteration mapping, this study pinpoints the precise ore-forming stages and delineates detailed alteration zoning features in the Tongshankou Cu–Mo–W deposit. Building on SWIR and TIR spectral parameters, alongside volumetric bulk magnetic susceptibility (Kbulk ) measurements, we propose four mineral exploration indicators for this deposit: (i) the widespread development of kaolinite, dickite, skarn, and retrograde alteration mineral assemblages; (ii) Fe-rich chlorite, identifiable by its characteristic absorption wavelength with Pos2250 > 2250 nm; (iii) white mica–montmorillonite, distinguished by a diagnostic absorption wavelength with Pos2200 1.0 × 10-3SI). Notably, the presence of kaolinite and dickite along the intrusive contact zones between ore-forming porphyries and carbonate wall rocks provides direct evidence for acidic fluid activity during the Cu–Mo–W mineralization process. item.title: Formation of Cu‐Bearing and Mo‐Bearing Miarolitic Cavities During the Magmatic–Hydrothermal Transition in Porphyry Metallogenic Systems
item.dc:creator: Chu Wu
Zhaoxia Liao
Tao Hong
Zuo Ge
Xiao Zheng
Di Liu
Xingwang Xu
item.description: ABSTRACT Miarolitic cavities (MCs) serve as key indicators of volatile phase exsolution during magmatic differentiation, providing critical insights into the transition between igneous and hydrothermal regimes. Despite their metallogenic significance, the mechanisms controlling the selective extraction of metal‐bearing fluids remain poorly understood. This study investigates the microstructural and geochemical characteristics of chalcopyrite‐ and molybdenite‐dominated MCs in the Hongyuan porphyry Mo–Cu deposit (NW China). The cavities exhibit sulfide cores surrounded by graphic quartz‐feldspar, aplitic, and K‐alteration halos. Mass balance calculations reveal enrichments in Cu, Fe, Na, and Si during the magmatic–hydrothermal transition. The term “sulfide‐silicate bulb” refers to silica‐saturated fluid pockets or bubbles that develop during hypogene copper mineralization in porphyry deposits. The central sulfide‐silicate bulbs represent hypersolidus mineral assemblages, with differential Cu–Mo precipitation linked to quartz growth rates. Late‐stage meteoric water alteration further influences metal precipitation. We propose a genetic model wherein sulfide‐silicate bulbs act as the primary vehicles for the extraction, transport, and focused precipitation of Cu‐ and Mo‐rich fluids. Our results highlight the critical role of quartz growth rates and meteoric fluid influx in controlling metal segregation, providing a new framework for understanding the spatial zonation in porphyry Cu–Mo systems. Published: 2026-07-01T16:50:02+00:00 Venue: Resource Geology Score: 0.591 (consider)

ABSTRACT Miarolitic cavities (MCs) serve as key indicators of volatile phase exsolution during magmatic differentiation, providing critical insights into the transition between igneous and hydrothermal regimes. Despite their metallogenic significance, the mechanisms controlling the selective extraction of metal‐bearing fluids remain poorly understood. This study investigates the microstructural and geochemical characteristics of chalcopyrite‐ and molybdenite‐dominated MCs in the Hongyuan porphyry Mo–Cu deposit (NW China). The cavities exhibit sulfide cores surrounded by graphic quartz‐feldspar, aplitic, and K‐alteration halos. Mass balance calculations reveal enrichments in Cu, Fe, Na, and Si during the magmatic–hydrothermal transition. The term “sulfide‐silicate bulb” refers to silica‐saturated fluid pockets or bubbles that develop during hypogene copper mineralization in porphyry deposits. The central sulfide‐silicate bulbs represent hypersolidus mineral assemblages, with differential Cu–Mo precipitation linked to quartz growth rates. Late‐stage meteoric water alteration further influences metal precipitation. We propose a genetic model wherein sulfide‐silicate bulbs act as the primary vehicles for the extraction, transport, and focused precipitation of Cu‐ and Mo‐rich fluids. Our results highlight the critical role of quartz growth rates and meteoric fluid influx in controlling metal segregation, providing a new framework for understanding the spatial zonation in porphyry Cu–Mo systems.

item.title: Reassessing long-term exhumation rates in magmatic terranes
item.dc:creator: F.J. Cooper
B.A. Adams
S.I.R. Dahlström
T.A. Ehlers
M.C. van Soest
K.V. Hodges
B.R. Jicha
B.S. Singer
J. Cortes Yañez
R.J. Perkins
item.description: Crustal exhumation is central to mountain building, weathering, and sediment production processes, which significantly influence the composition and behavior of Earth’s oceans and atmosphere. It also controls the formation, enrichment, and preservation of porphyry copper deposits, which are a vital source of metals for the clean energy technologies underpinning the global green energy transition. Exhumation rates are typically determined from rock cooling histories combined with a geothermal model. However, in magmatically active regions, models can overpredict exhumation rates because the elevated heat signature is mistakenly attributed to advection driven by rapid exhumation. We present a method to accurately constrain exhumation histories in magmatic terranes that combines thermokinematic modeling of thermochronometric cooling histories with independent geobarometric constraints on the total exhumed crustal thickness. Focusing on the Central Andes, which has a prolonged history of magmatic and magmatic-hydrothermal activity, we find that accounting for this additional source of heat results in exhumed crustal thicknesses within error of the independent constraints, whereas ignoring it results in exhumed thicknesses up to four times greater than geologically plausible. Published: 2026-07-07T04:39:43+00:00 Venue: Earth and Planetary Science Letters Score: 0.591 (consider)

Authors: F.J. Cooper; B.A. Adams; S.I.R. Dahlström; T.A. Ehlers; M.C. van Soest; K.V. Hodges; B.R. Jicha; B.S. Singer; J. Cortes Yañez; R.J. Perkins

Journal: Earth and Planetary Science Letters

DOI: 10.1016/j.epsl.2026.120217

Relevance: 0.591 (consider)

Crustal exhumation is central to mountain building, weathering, and sediment production processes, which significantly influence the composition and behavior of Earth’s oceans and atmosphere. It also controls the formation, enrichment, and preservation of porphyry copper deposits, which are a vital source of metals for the clean energy technologies underpinning the global green energy transition. Exhumation rates are typically determined from rock cooling histories combined with a geothermal model. However, in magmatically active regions, models can overpredict exhumation rates because the elevated heat signature is mistakenly attributed to advection driven by rapid exhumation. We present a method to accurately constrain exhumation histories in magmatic terranes that combines thermokinematic modeling of thermochronometric cooling histories with independent geobarometric constraints on the total exhumed crustal thickness. Focusing on the Central Andes, which has a prolonged history of magmatic and magmatic-hydrothermal activity, we find that accounting for this additional source of heat results in exhumed crustal thicknesses within error of the independent constraints, whereas ignoring it results in exhumed thicknesses up to four times greater than geologically plausible.

item.title: Geochronology and genesis of rare-metal mineralization in the Himalayan leucogranite-pegmatite system
item.dc:creator: Menglan Ran
Xiaoming Sun
Ying Liu
Zhengpeng Ding
Guangming Li
item.description: Himalayan leucogranites are a key target for rare-metal exploration, yet the sources and petrogenetic evolution of their parental magmas remain debated. This study investigates three representative deposits hosted by leucogranite-pegmatite systems in distinct tectonic units of the Himalayan orogen, integrating apatite and cassiterite U-Pb geochronology, whole-rock and apatite geochemistry, and apatite oxygen isotopes. Combined cassiterite and apatite dating reveals that rare-metal mineralization developed through multiple discrete magmatic-hydrothermal pulses rather than a single event, defining a prolonged, multi-stage metallogenic framework. Whole-rock and apatite trace-element signatures indicate highly fractionated, relatively oxidized melts that experienced extensive fluid exsolution, consistent with derivation from ancient metasedimentary sources within the Higher Himalayan Crystalline Sequence. These pegmatites likely evolved from parental leucogranitic magmas through extreme fractional crystallization and fluid exsolution. Apatite oxygen isotopes further resolve three contrasting fluid-evolution paths among the deposits. These range from open-system overprinting by external fluids, through continuous internal magmatic-to-hydrothermal evolution, to a largely closed system dominated by extreme fractionation, with each path corresponding to a distinct mineralization style. Collectively, these data suggest that post-collisional crustal decompression promoted partial melting of metasedimentary rocks, and the resultant leucogranitic magmas underwent high-degree fractionation and fluid exsolution to form rare-metal-bearing pegmatites. These findings constrain crustal melting, magmatic differentiation, and rare-metal enrichment in the Himalayan collisional orogen and highlight apatite as a sensitive tracer of pegmatite-forming processes. Published: 2026-07-03T06:44:11+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.588 (consider)

Himalayan leucogranites are a key target for rare-metal exploration, yet the sources and petrogenetic evolution of their parental magmas remain debated. This study investigates three representative deposits hosted by leucogranite-pegmatite systems in distinct tectonic units of the Himalayan orogen, integrating apatite and cassiterite U-Pb geochronology, whole-rock and apatite geochemistry, and apatite oxygen isotopes. Combined cassiterite and apatite dating reveals that rare-metal mineralization developed through multiple discrete magmatic-hydrothermal pulses rather than a single event, defining a prolonged, multi-stage metallogenic framework. Whole-rock and apatite trace-element signatures indicate highly fractionated, relatively oxidized melts that experienced extensive fluid exsolution, consistent with derivation from ancient metasedimentary sources within the Higher Himalayan Crystalline Sequence. These pegmatites likely evolved from parental leucogranitic magmas through extreme fractional crystallization and fluid exsolution. Apatite oxygen isotopes further resolve three contrasting fluid-evolution paths among the deposits. These range from open-system overprinting by external fluids, through continuous internal magmatic-to-hydrothermal evolution, to a largely closed system dominated by extreme fractionation, with each path corresponding to a distinct mineralization style. Collectively, these data suggest that post-collisional crustal decompression promoted partial melting of metasedimentary rocks, and the resultant leucogranitic magmas underwent high-degree fractionation and fluid exsolution to form rare-metal-bearing pegmatites. These findings constrain crustal melting, magmatic differentiation, and rare-metal enrichment in the Himalayan collisional orogen and highlight apatite as a sensitive tracer of pegmatite-forming processes.

item.title: Fingerprints of s-cl-bearing fluids deduced from metal and mineral microinclusions in Quaternary Kamchatka lavas
item.dc:creator: Pavel Kepezhinskas
Nikolai Berdnikov
Nadezhda Potapova
Valeria Krutikova
item.description: Quaternary ankaramite, basalt, andesite and dacite from the Avacha, Bakening, Mutnovsky and Gorely volcanoes in Kamchatka contain numerous mineral microinclusions in rock-forming silicate and accessory oxide minerals as well as in groundmass volcanic glass. We have studied the composition and textural setting of these microinclusions along with the petrology and geochemictsry of their host lavas using optical petrography, major and trace element geochemistry and scanning electron microscopy with energy dispersive spectroscopy (SEM-EDS). The microinclusions are represented by Au and Au-bearing alloys, Ag and Ag-bearing alloys, partially oxidized siderophile (W) and chalcophile (Pb, Zn, Cu, Bi, Sb, Pb) metals. S-Cl-bearing microinclusions as Ag (± Cu) chlorides, composite S-O-C-bearing metal chlorides, Ag (±Cu) sulfides, sulfides of Fe, Ni, Cu, Pb, Zn and Sb as well as barite, cassiterite and apatite are very characteristic for these rocks. Primary-textured, magmatic microinclusions in olivine, pyroxenes, amphibole, plagioclase and fresh volcanic glass show no evidence for the later-stage transformations. Secondary-textured, post-magmatic microinclusions are localized in pores, voids and fractures or along the boundaries of silicate minerals and in association with secondary minerals. Magmatic and post-magmatic S-Cl-bearing microinclusion assemblages almost completely overlap compositionally suggesting that S-Cl-bearing fluids accompanied formation of Kamchatka lavas from magmatic differentiation in sub-volcanic conduits to their emplacement, cooling and degassing above the surface as well as possible post-eruptive transformations near active fumarolic fields. Lavas from the Avacha, Bakening, Mutnovsky and Gorely volcanoes display elevated Pb, Zn, Sb, Cu and Ag concentrations in comparison with the average MORB and upper continental crust. Some volcanic samples with both magmatic and post-magmatic S-Cl-bearing mineral microinclusions have higher Mo, Sn, Sb and Ag contents than previously reported for Kamchatka volcanoes suggesting that sulfur and chlorine potentially contribute to metal enrichment in magmas and associated fluids and facilitate transport of certain ore metals in the Kamchatka subduction zone. Published: 2026-07-04T15:23:16+00:00 Venue: Geoscience Frontiers Score: 0.588 (consider)

Journal: Geoscience Frontiers

Quaternary ankaramite, basalt, andesite and dacite from the Avacha, Bakening, Mutnovsky and Gorely volcanoes in Kamchatka contain numerous mineral microinclusions in rock-forming silicate and accessory oxide minerals as well as in groundmass volcanic glass. We have studied the composition and textural setting of these microinclusions along with the petrology and geochemictsry of their host lavas using optical petrography, major and trace element geochemistry and scanning electron microscopy with energy dispersive spectroscopy (SEM-EDS). The microinclusions are represented by Au and Au-bearing alloys, Ag and Ag-bearing alloys, partially oxidized siderophile (W) and chalcophile (Pb, Zn, Cu, Bi, Sb, Pb) metals. S-Cl-bearing microinclusions as Ag (± Cu) chlorides, composite S-O-C-bearing metal chlorides, Ag (±Cu) sulfides, sulfides of Fe, Ni, Cu, Pb, Zn and Sb as well as barite, cassiterite and apatite are very characteristic for these rocks. Primary-textured, magmatic microinclusions in olivine, pyroxenes, amphibole, plagioclase and fresh volcanic glass show no evidence for the later-stage transformations. Secondary-textured, post-magmatic microinclusions are localized in pores, voids and fractures or along the boundaries of silicate minerals and in association with secondary minerals. Magmatic and post-magmatic S-Cl-bearing microinclusion assemblages almost completely overlap compositionally suggesting that S-Cl-bearing fluids accompanied formation of Kamchatka lavas from magmatic differentiation in sub-volcanic conduits to their emplacement, cooling and degassing above the surface as well as possible post-eruptive transformations near active fumarolic fields. Lavas from the Avacha, Bakening, Mutnovsky and Gorely volcanoes display elevated Pb, Zn, Sb, Cu and Ag concentrations in comparison with the average MORB and upper continental crust. Some volcanic samples with both magmatic and post-magmatic S-Cl-bearing mineral microinclusions have higher Mo, Sn, Sb and Ag contents than previously reported for Kamchatka volcanoes suggesting that sulfur and chlorine potentially contribute to metal enrichment in magmas and associated fluids and facilitate transport of certain ore metals in the Kamchatka subduction zone.

item.title: Geochemical, mineralogical, and geochronological constraints on the Wugongshan granite-aplite complex: Implications for regional Li-Nb-Ta mineralization in South China
item.dc:creator: Xiaodong Ma
Bin Wu
Yinqiu Hu
Guanglai Li
Bo Peng
Kai Xu
Yuehu Lyu
item.description: A key obstacle to understanding rare-metal mineralization in highly evolved granitic systems is the uncertain petrogenetic relationship between granites and their felsic derivatives. This study presents an integrated investigation of Mesozoic granites and aplite dikes from the Wugongshan complex in South China, which incorporates whole-rock geochemistry, mineral composition, U-Pb dating of apatite and cassiterite, and apatite Sr isotope. Both rock types crystallized at ∼ 151–150 Ma. Relative to barren granites, the Li-Nb-Ta mineralized aplite are characterized by the crystallization of lepidolite, columbite-(Mn), tantalite-(Mn), and microlite. Geochemical and mineralogical evidence indicates that the aplite crystallized from a more evolved, fluid-rich melts during the magmatic-hydrothermal transition stage. However, mineralogical and Sr isotopic discrepancies between the granites (whole-rock initial 87Sr/86Sr = 0.7143–0.7208, from literature) and the aplite (apatite initial 87Sr/86Sr = 0.7512–0.7648) preclude a simple in situ differentiation origin for the aplite from the exposed granites. Instead, it probably originated from a more reduced, isotopically and compositionally heterogeneous underlying batch, either derived from a different deep-seated magma chamber or from the same chamber that sourced the exposed granites but subsequently underwent crustal assimilation at depth. Both alternative models are characterized by high initial 87Sr/86Sr ratios. Regional comparisons with Yichun and Yifeng deposits imply that rare-metal granite-aplite systems in western Jiangxi may result from multiple episodes of granitic magmatism, reflecting heterogeneous source melting and multistage magma recharge on a regional scale, rather than being sole products of continuous fractional crystallization and fluid exsolution. Published: 2026-07-03T23:07:49+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.585 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xiaodong Ma; Bin Wu; Yinqiu Hu; Guanglai Li; Bo Peng; Kai Xu; Yuehu Lyu

Journal: Ore Geology Reviews

DOI: 10.1016/j.oregeorev.2026.107422

Relevance: 0.585 (consider)

A key obstacle to understanding rare-metal mineralization in highly evolved granitic systems is the uncertain petrogenetic relationship between granites and their felsic derivatives. This study presents an integrated investigation of Mesozoic granites and aplite dikes from the Wugongshan complex in South China, which incorporates whole-rock geochemistry, mineral composition, U-Pb dating of apatite and cassiterite, and apatite Sr isotope. Both rock types crystallized at ∼ 151–150 Ma. Relative to barren granites, the Li-Nb-Ta mineralized aplite are characterized by the crystallization of lepidolite, columbite-(Mn), tantalite-(Mn), and microlite. Geochemical and mineralogical evidence indicates that the aplite crystallized from a more evolved, fluid-rich melts during the magmatic-hydrothermal transition stage. However, mineralogical and Sr isotopic discrepancies between the granites (whole-rock initial 87Sr/86Sr = 0.7143–0.7208, from literature) and the aplite (apatite initial 87Sr/86Sr = 0.7512–0.7648) preclude a simple in situ differentiation origin for the aplite from the exposed granites. Instead, it probably originated from a more reduced, isotopically and compositionally heterogeneous underlying batch, either derived from a different deep-seated magma chamber or from the same chamber that sourced the exposed granites but subsequently underwent crustal assimilation at depth. Both alternative models are characterized by high initial 87Sr/86Sr ratios. Regional comparisons with Yichun and Yifeng deposits imply that rare-metal granite-aplite systems in western Jiangxi may result from multiple episodes of granitic magmatism, reflecting heterogeneous source melting and multistage magma recharge on a regional scale, rather than being sole products of continuous fractional crystallization and fluid exsolution.

item.title: Subsolidus fluid generation via coupled muscovite dehydroxylation and biotite dehydration: Experimental constraints for metasomatism in granites
item.dc:creator: Maria Monika Repczyńska
Aitor Cambeses
José Francisco Molina
Antonio Garcia-Casco
Pilar Montero
María Salguero-Fuentes
Leticia Barcos
Josefina Pedrajas-Jurado
Fernando Bea
item.description: Subsolidus muscovite dehydroxylation represents a significant internal fluid source in felsic rocks. To investigate its effects, non-equilibrium differential-heating experiments were conducted at 1 bar and 750–1000 °C, together with piston–cylinder experiments at 800 °C and 500 MPa, using a two-mica orthogneiss and a biotite orthogneiss. The two lithologies show markedly different behaviour. In the biotite orthogneiss, only limited transformations occur. In contrast, muscovite dehydroxylation in the two-mica orthogneiss generates internally derived H2O fluids capable of reactivating mineral-replacement reactions without external fluid addition. Structurally bound OH released during dehydroxylation destabilises adjacent biotite, producing fine-grained orthopyroxene + spinel aggregates accompanied by the loss of H2O, K, Ti and F. The generated fluids also promote titanite growth and pervasive alkali metasomatism. Plagioclase is replaced by transitional Or–Ab feldspar, porous albite, and newly formed K-feldspar, producing textures comparable to those in natural hydrothermal and lower-crustal metasomatic systems. These microstructures indicate efficient fluid-mediated mass transfer even at extremely low fluid abundances (<1 wt%). The contrasting responses of the two orthogneisses highlight the role of muscovite in controlling fluid availability, alkali mobility, and subsolidus reaction pathways. A major consequence is the transfer of the K-feldspar component hosted in biotite into newly formed K-feldspar, shifting the bulk composition towards granite and potentially enhancing fertility during subsequent water-fluxed melting. These experiments demonstrate that mica-derived fluids can act as effective metasomatic agents during thermal shock events, linking dehydration reactions, feldspar re-equilibration, and the preconditioning of felsic rocks for anatexis. Published: 2026-07-02T23:20:18+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.584 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Maria Monika Repczyńska; Aitor Cambeses; José Francisco Molina; Antonio Garcia-Casco; Pilar Montero; María Salguero-Fuentes; Leticia Barcos; Josefina Pedrajas-Jurado; Fernando Bea

Journal: Lithos

DOI: 10.1016/j.lithos.2026.108703

Relevance: 0.584 (consider)

Subsolidus muscovite dehydroxylation represents a significant internal fluid source in felsic rocks. To investigate its effects, non-equilibrium differential-heating experiments were conducted at 1 bar and 750–1000 °C, together with piston–cylinder experiments at 800 °C and 500 MPa, using a two-mica orthogneiss and a biotite orthogneiss. The two lithologies show markedly different behaviour. In the biotite orthogneiss, only limited transformations occur. In contrast, muscovite dehydroxylation in the two-mica orthogneiss generates internally derived H2O fluids capable of reactivating mineral-replacement reactions without external fluid addition. Structurally bound OH released during dehydroxylation destabilises adjacent biotite, producing fine-grained orthopyroxene + spinel aggregates accompanied by the loss of H2O, K, Ti and F. The generated fluids also promote titanite growth and pervasive alkali metasomatism. Plagioclase is replaced by transitional Or–Ab feldspar, porous albite, and newly formed K-feldspar, producing textures comparable to those in natural hydrothermal and lower-crustal metasomatic systems. These microstructures indicate efficient fluid-mediated mass transfer even at extremely low fluid abundances (<1 wt%). The contrasting responses of the two orthogneisses highlight the role of muscovite in controlling fluid availability, alkali mobility, and subsolidus reaction pathways. A major consequence is the transfer of the K-feldspar component hosted in biotite into newly formed K-feldspar, shifting the bulk composition towards granite and potentially enhancing fertility during subsequent water-fluxed melting. These experiments demonstrate that mica-derived fluids can act as effective metasomatic agents during thermal shock events, linking dehydration reactions, feldspar re-equilibration, and the preconditioning of felsic rocks for anatexis.

item.title: Regional-scale integrated 3D modeling of the northern Norrbotten ore province (Sweden): Implications on the iron oxide-apatite mineral system
item.dc:creator: Ervin Veress
Oskar Rydman
Tobias E. Bauer
Joel BH. Andersson
Maxim Y. Smirnov
Thorkild M. Rasmussen
Irvine R. Annesley
item.description: The northern Norrbotten ore province, situated within the Fennoscandian Shield, constitutes a major metallogenic domain in Europe and hosts more than 40 iron oxide-apatite (IOA) deposits, including the Kiirunavaara deposit, the type locality for this deposit type. Although extensive mapping, geochemical, geochronological, and structural studies have refined the conceptual understanding of IOA systems at deposit and district scales, a fully integrated three-dimensional framework that synthesizes this knowledge with regional geological and geophysical datasets has not yet been developed. This study presents an integrated 3D model of the northern Norrbotten ore province that incorporates lithological, structural, and petrophysical constraints with inversions of magnetic and gravity data, as well as available magnetotelluric conductivity models. The geological models delineate the architecture of intrusive suites, supracrustal belts, crustal-scale shear zones, and subordinate brittle–ductile second-order structures, implemented in a voxel-based domain for quantitative spatial integration. Mafic to ultramafic intrusions previously linked locally to IOA genesis are consistently coincident with high-density, magnetically anomalous domains and spatially associated with vertically extensive electrical conductors. These features are interpreted as the upper-crustal expression of a deeper transcrustal conduit system that facilitated magma ascent, fluid migration, and heat transfer. IOA deposits are located within second-order oblique structures intersecting or splaying from first-order structures, and their geometry and extent correlate with diagnostic magnetic signatures characterized by steep gradients, localized highs, and adjacent negative side lobes. These findings refine the metallogenic framework for IOA mineralization in the northern Norrbotten ore province and provide a robust 3D geoscientific basis for testing mineral system hypotheses. Published: 2026-07-04T22:23:15+00:00 Venue: Geoscience Frontiers Score: 0.583 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Ervin Veress; Oskar Rydman; Tobias E. Bauer; Joel BH. Andersson; Maxim Y. Smirnov; Thorkild M. Rasmussen; Irvine R. Annesley

Journal: Geoscience Frontiers

DOI: 10.1016/j.gsf.2026.102404

Relevance: 0.583 (consider)

The northern Norrbotten ore province, situated within the Fennoscandian Shield, constitutes a major metallogenic domain in Europe and hosts more than 40 iron oxide-apatite (IOA) deposits, including the Kiirunavaara deposit, the type locality for this deposit type. Although extensive mapping, geochemical, geochronological, and structural studies have refined the conceptual understanding of IOA systems at deposit and district scales, a fully integrated three-dimensional framework that synthesizes this knowledge with regional geological and geophysical datasets has not yet been developed. This study presents an integrated 3D model of the northern Norrbotten ore province that incorporates lithological, structural, and petrophysical constraints with inversions of magnetic and gravity data, as well as available magnetotelluric conductivity models. The geological models delineate the architecture of intrusive suites, supracrustal belts, crustal-scale shear zones, and subordinate brittle–ductile second-order structures, implemented in a voxel-based domain for quantitative spatial integration. Mafic to ultramafic intrusions previously linked locally to IOA genesis are consistently coincident with high-density, magnetically anomalous domains and spatially associated with vertically extensive electrical conductors. These features are interpreted as the upper-crustal expression of a deeper transcrustal conduit system that facilitated magma ascent, fluid migration, and heat transfer. IOA deposits are located within second-order oblique structures intersecting or splaying from first-order structures, and their geometry and extent correlate with diagnostic magnetic signatures characterized by steep gradients, localized highs, and adjacent negative side lobes. These findings refine the metallogenic framework for IOA mineralization in the northern Norrbotten ore province and provide a robust 3D geoscientific basis for testing mineral system hypotheses.

item.title: Vanadium isotopes demonstrate depth-dependent magma differentiation in Rindjani Volcano, Sunda arc
item.dc:creator: Xin Ding
Min Ji
Weixin Lv
Fang Huang
Erik V. Sørensen
Paul M. Holm
John D. Foden
item.description: The andesitic composition of the continental crust—characterized by high SiO2 and low FeOT contents—has been attributed to differentiation processes operating in arc settings. One contributing mechanism could be amphibole-forming peritectic reaction at the crust–mantle boundary; however, its role in arc magma differentiation remains poorly constrained. Here we present high-precision δ 51/50V data (hereafter δ51V) for volcanic rocks from the Rindjani suite in the Sunda arc that span compositions from high-Al basalts to trachydacites, investigating how V isotopes respond to amphibole-controlled magma differentiation. At the crust–mantle boundary, amphibole-forming peritectic reactions transform H2O-rich magma into Si-rich melt and induce minor δ51V variation of approximately 0.08‰. H2O-rich magmas that bypass the peritectic reaction ascend to the shallow crust and differentiate from high-Al basalts to high-K andesites, showing limited δ51V variation from −0.76‰ to −0.66‰. Shallow-level differentiation of the Si-rich melt from andesites to trachydacites produces a sharp increase in δ51V from −0.62‰ to −0.04‰, consistent with an average mineral-melt fractionation factor Δ51Vmineral–melt ≈ −0.30‰. Published Fe−Ti isotope data show that δ56Fe remained constant, whereas δ49Ti responded to differentiation throughout the compositional range of the Rindjani suite. The decoupled Fe–Ti–V isotope systematics reveal differences in elemental mass balance and mineral–melt isotope fractionation during magma differentiation. These results show that amphibole-forming peritectic reactions fundamentally modify melt chemistry, thereby enabling significant V isotope fractionation associated with magnetite crystallization at shallow levels. Our findings highlight the depth–dependent differentiation framework of arc magmas and advance understanding of the V isotopic pathways involved in continental crust formation. Published: 2026-07-05T21:55:12+00:00 Venue: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta Score: 0.582 (consider)

The andesitic composition of the continental crust—characterized by high SiO2 and low FeOT contents—has been attributed to differentiation processes operating in arc settings. Our findings highlight the depth–dependent differentiation framework of arc magmas and advance understanding of the V isotopic pathways involved in continental crust formation.

item.title: Garnet amphibolite as relics of TTG magma: Evidence from the Wuhe complex in the eastern North China Craton
item.dc:creator: Hai-Yan Su
Yang Yang
Yi-Can Liu
Chris Yakymchuk
Peng Peng
item.description: Tonalite–trondhjemite–granodiorite (TTG) gneisses constitute the dominant component of cratons. Their parental rocks are thought to be continental mafic rocks; however, direct field evidence remains scarce. A suite of garnet amphibolite from the Wuhe Complex in the North China Craton provides direct evidence of in situ melting based on integrated field observation, whole-rock geochemistry, zircon UPb geochronology and phase equilibrium and trace-element modelling. Zircon UPb data indicate that the amphibolites underwent anatexis at 1.95–2.1 Ga, followed by a retrograde overprint at ca. 1.8 Ga. Bulk compositions of the leucosome are similar to those typical TTGs in the adjacent region; while those of the amphibolite fit a relic composition after the derivation of the leucosome. Phase equilibrium modelling constrains the peak melting to produce TTG-like melt such as the leucosome in the amphibolite to medium-pressure conditions (~9 kbar and ~ 840 °C). Trace-element modelling shows that the compositions of parental rocks and the residual mineral phases (garnet, plagioclase, amphibole, and rutile) in them controlled the elemental patterns in the melt. This study confirms that the TTGs can be produced from mafic crust at relatively low pressure conditions inside the continental crust. Published: 2026-07-04T15:07:55+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.578 (consider)

Journal: Lithos

DOI: 10.1016/j.lithos.2026.108704

Relevance: 0.578 (consider)

Tonalite–trondhjemite–granodiorite (TTG) gneisses constitute the dominant component of cratons. Their parental rocks are thought to be continental mafic rocks; however, direct field evidence remains scarce. A suite of garnet amphibolite from the Wuhe Complex in the North China Craton provides direct evidence of in situ melting based on integrated field observation, whole-rock geochemistry, zircon UPb geochronology and phase equilibrium and trace-element modelling. Zircon UPb data indicate that the amphibolites underwent anatexis at 1.95–2.1 Ga, followed by a retrograde overprint at ca. 1.8 Ga. Bulk compositions of the leucosome are similar to those typical TTGs in the adjacent region; while those of the amphibolite fit a relic composition after the derivation of the leucosome. Phase equilibrium modelling constrains the peak melting to produce TTG-like melt such as the leucosome in the amphibolite to medium-pressure conditions (~9 kbar and ~ 840 °C). Trace-element modelling shows that the compositions of parental rocks and the residual mineral phases (garnet, plagioclase, amphibole, and rutile) in them controlled the elemental patterns in the melt. This study confirms that the TTGs can be produced from mafic crust at relatively low pressure conditions inside the continental crust.

item.title: Slab geometry controls Paleo-Tethyan post-collisional tectonomagmatism: Insights from Late Triassic ultrapotassic rocks in Peninsular Malaysia
item.dc:creator: Yao Xiao
Xi-Jun Liu
Hao Wu
Jun-Hong Zhao
Wei Fu
Lei Liu
Sai-Sai Li
Jin-Bao Yang
Li-Yan Ma
Yun Zhou
Ya Qin
item.description: Post-collisional ultrapotassic magmas are generally formed by partial melting of a subduction-metasomatized mantle source in response to continental collision and subduction. This paper presents new geochronological, geochemical, and Sr−Nd−Hf−Pb isotopic data for a suite of potassic and ultrapotassic rocks from the Raub Complex in Peninsular Malaysia. Zircon U−Pb dating shows the ultrapotassic rocks were emplaced at 225−222 Ma and the potassic rocks at 216 Ma. The elemental and isotopic data suggest the ultrapotassic rocks were derived from melt-metasomatized, subcontinental lithospheric mantle, and the potassic rocks were derived from subducted continental sediments. The occurrence of the ultrapotassic rocks, along with spatiotemporal variations in the geochemistry of igneous rocks in Peninsular Malaysia, indicate that continental collision began at ca. 235 Ma, followed by progressive shallow continental subduction and slab breakoff at ca. 225 Ma. Two distinct post-collisional tectonomagmatic events occurred along the eastern Paleo-Tethyan suture, characterized by rapid exhumation associated with the formation of low-K mafic rocks and limited exhumation associated with ultrapotassic rocks. We propose that high- and low-angle continental subduction can account for these different post-collisional events. The results provide insights into the Late Triassic geological evolution of the eastern Paleo-Tethyan suture and enhance our understanding of the effect of slab geometry on post-collisional tectonomagmatism. Published: 2026-07-06T14:00:16+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.577 (consider)

Authors: Yao Xiao; Xi-Jun Liu; Hao Wu; Jun-Hong Zhao; Wei Fu; Lei Liu; Sai-Sai Li; Jin-Bao Yang; Li-Yan Ma; Yun Zhou; Ya Qin

Journal: Geological Society of America Bulletin
